h1Title: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) is a chronic neuropathic pain disorder characterised by disproportionate, burning pain; sensory, autonomic, and motor abnormalities; and trophic changes following a triggering injury to a limb. CRPS affects approximately 5-26 per 100,000 person-years in population studies and is most commonly triggered by fractures, sprains, surgeries, or immobilisation. Women are affected 3-4 times more often than men. The Budapest criteria, validated in multiple countries, are the current diagnostic standard: they require pain disproportionate to the injury along with specific sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor-oedema, and motor-trophic symptoms and signs. Treatment is multimodal, combining physiotherapy, neuromodulation, pharmacotherapy, and psychological support, but outcomes remain variable and full resolution occurs in roughly 74% of patients within 12 months when treated early.

keyStats.prevalence: 5-26 per 100,000 person-years; estimated 200,000 new cases per year in the United States (de Mos et al. 2007, Sandroni et al. 2003)
keyStats.demographics: Women are 3-4× more affected than men; peak incidence at age 50-70 years; upper limb more commonly affected than lower limb in most series
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at onset approximately 46-52 years in population studies; paediatric CRPS well documented, peak in adolescents aged 9-15
keyStats.globalCases: Global CRPS burden is poorly quantified; total prevalence estimated at 1.5-2 million cases in the US alone given the chronic course

definition: Complex regional pain syndrome (ICD-10: G90.5) is a chronic condition characterised by persistent pain disproportionate in intensity and duration to any inciting event, accompanied by sensory, autonomic, motor, and trophic abnormalities in one or more limbs. CRPS is classified into two types: CRPS type 1 (formerly reflex sympathetic dystrophy) occurs without identifiable nerve injury — accounting for 90% of cases; CRPS type 2 (formerly causalgia) is associated with confirmed peripheral nerve lesion. Both types share identical diagnostic criteria: the 2007 Budapest criteria require pain disproportionate to inciting injury, plus symptom and sign criteria across at least two of four categories: sensory (allodynia, hyperalgesia); vasomotor (temperature asymmetry, skin colour changes); sudomotor-oedema (sweating asymmetry, oedema); and motor-trophic (weakness, tremor, dystonia, abnormal hair or nail growth). The pathophysiology involves peripheral sensitisation of damaged nociceptors, central sensitisation in the spinal dorsal horn and cortex, dysregulation of the sympathetic nervous system causing aberrant noradrenaline sensitisation of nociceptors (sympathetically maintained pain), and neurogenic inflammation from mast cell and nociceptive fibre interaction. Immune and genetic factors are increasingly implicated. CRPS is primarily managed by pain medicine specialists, often within multidisciplinary pain management programmes, with rehabilitation medicine, psychology, and neurology input.

typesClassification.type: CRPS Type 1 (formerly reflex sympathetic dystrophy, RSD)
typesClassification.description: No identifiable nerve lesion. Accounts for approximately 90% of CRPS cases. Typically follows fracture (most commonly distal radius), sprain, surgery, or immobilisation. Identical clinical features to CRPS type 2.

Associated with a confirmed peripheral nerve injury. Pain distribution may be beyond the nerve territory. Carries a similar prognosis and treatment approach to type 1, though surgical decompression of the offending nerve may provide additional benefit in select cases.

Previously valid CRPS features not fully meeting Budapest criteria or showing some resolution but with ongoing pain and functional limitation. A useful diagnostic category when the full syndrome has partially remitted or criteria are borderline.

primarySymptoms: Burning, constant pain in the affected limb rated as severe — often described as feeling like the affected hand or foot is on fire — disproportionate to the original injury in intensity and duration.
Allodynia — pain produced by stimuli that are not normally painful, such as light touch of clothing, water temperature changes, or air movement — affecting the skin of the involved limb.
Hyperalgesia — exaggerated pain response to mildly painful stimuli, such as light pin-prick — in the territory of the affected limb.

Temperature asymmetry of the affected limb — initially the limb is often warm and red; in chronic disease it typically becomes cold, blue, and mottled — reflecting changes in sympathetic vasomotor tone.
Skin colour changes — erythema, cyanosis, or mottling — in the affected limb, often asymmetric and variable throughout the day.
Oedema of the affected extremity, typically pitting early in the disease, contributing to stiffness and functional impairment.
Sweating abnormalities — hyperhidrosis or anhidrosis of the affected limb compared with the contralateral side.
Motor dysfunction: weakness, tremor, and reduced range of motion out of proportion to voluntary effort; fixed dystonia (involuntary sustained posturing of fingers or foot) in chronic severe cases.
Trophic changes: abnormal hair and nail growth (either accelerated early or sparse and brittle in chronic disease); skin atrophy or thickening; muscle wasting in the affected limb.

Psychological distress — depression, anxiety, kinesiophobia (fear of movement), and catastrophising occur in a high proportion of CRPS patients and amplify functional disability beyond the direct pain mechanism.
earlyWarningSigns: Limb pain that is strikingly disproportionate in severity and duration to the original fracture or sprain — warning sign for early CRPS during initial orthopaedic review.
New onset sweating asymmetry, skin warmth, and oedema in an injured limb beyond 4-6 weeks post-injury — early autonomic changes.
Pain that worsens rather than improves with elevation or movement during normal fracture recovery — opposite to typical musculoskeletal healing trajectory.
emergencySigns: Rapidly spreading skin colour changes, high fever, and systemic inflammatory markers in an affected limb — raise concern for septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, or deep tissue infection requiring urgent imaging and surgical review.

Fixed limb dystonia with loss of all voluntary movement and severe contracture developing over days — requires urgent pain medicine and rehabilitation review to prevent permanent structural deformity.
Severe psychiatric crisis (suicidal ideation) in a patient with refractory CRPS — reported in up to 20% of severe chronic CRPS cases; requires same-day mental health referral.

causes.cause: Fracture — especially distal radius and ankle
causes.description: Fracture is the most common trigger, accounting for 40-45% of CRPS cases. The distal radius (Colles fracture) is the single most common precursor injury. Fracture-related CRPS may be triggered by direct nerve and tissue damage, immobilisation, or the inflammatory response to injury.

Surgical procedures on the hand, wrist, knee, and foot are implicated in CRPS development. Prolonged tourniquet use, tight plaster cast immobilisation, and post-operative oedema are procedural risk factors. Carpal tunnel release has a recognised association with subsequent CRPS.

causes.cause: Sprain, soft-tissue injury, or crush injury
causes.description: Minor sprains and contusions trigger CRPS in susceptible individuals — sometimes the clinical severity of the original injury is so minor that it is overlooked, reinforcing the concept of disproportionate response. Crush injuries cause more widespread tissue damage and carry higher CRPS risk.

Peripheral nociceptor sensitisation from neurogenic inflammation is amplified by central dorsal-horn sensitisation through NMDA-receptor upregulation and loss of inhibitory interneuron function. This central sensitisation sustains and generalises pain independently of ongoing peripheral input.

In a subset of patients (sympathetically maintained pain), noradrenaline released from sympathetic fibres directly activates alpha-adrenergic receptors on sensitised nociceptors, generating pain. This is demonstrated by relief with sympathetic blockade. In sympathetically independent pain, central mechanisms dominate and sympatholytic procedures are less effective.

riskFactors.description: Women are 3-4× more affected than men in population cohorts; hormonal factors and higher rates of central sensitisation in women are proposed mechanisms.
Peak incidence in older adults, likely related to greater likelihood of fracture, slower inflammatory resolution, and higher background sensitisation.

Colles fracture carries the highest absolute CRPS risk of any specific injury — approximately 7-37% of distal radius fractures develop CRPS signs in prospective studies; severe cases evolve in a smaller proportion.
Prolonged cast immobilisation, traction, and restricted mobilisation are consistently associated with CRPS development. Early active physiotherapy during fracture recovery is protective.

Catastrophising (magnified negative expectations) and anxiety independently predict CRPS development and poor outcome. Psychological vulnerability does not cause CRPS but lowers the sensitisation threshold.
Personal history of CRPS increases recurrence risk substantially; the same sensitisation substrate predisposes to CRPS in other limbs after subsequent injuries.
Observational data suggest vitamin D deficiency is associated with worse CRPS outcomes; supplementation to normal levels is a low-risk intervention with possible benefit.

affectedDemographics: Women account for 75-80% of CRPS cases in population-based studies (Sandroni et al. 2003, de Mos et al. 2007).
Upper limb CRPS is slightly more common than lower limb across most series; the wrist and hand account for approximately 45-50% of cases.
Paediatric CRPS — predominantly in girls aged 9-15 — presents with lower-extremity involvement in 80% of cases and has a generally better prognosis than adult CRPS.
Lower income groups and those with greater work-related injury exposure have higher CRPS prevalence in some occupational cohort studies.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical, based on the 2007 Budapest criteria endorsed by the International Association for the Study of Pain (IASP) and validated internationally. The criteria require pain disproportionate to any inciting event, plus at least one symptom and one sign in three or more of four categories: sensory (reports allodynia or hyperalgesia; signs of allodynia or hyperalgesia on examination); vasomotor (reports temperature asymmetry or skin colour change; signs of temperature or colour asymmetry); sudomotor/oedema (reports sweating change or oedema; signs of sweating asymmetry or oedema); motor/trophic (reports weakness, tremor, dystonia, or trophic changes; signs of these). Diagnosis must not be better explained by another condition. The diagnostic sensitivity of Budapest criteria is approximately 99%; specificity is 68%. Objective supporting investigations include: three-phase bone scintigraphy showing peri-articular uptake in phase 3 (sensitive, not specific); quantitative sensory testing (QST) demonstrating sensory abnormalities; skin temperature measurement with infrared thermometry (asymmetry >1°C); and plain radiographs demonstrating Sudeck's atrophy (periarticular bone loss) in chronic disease. MRI may show soft-tissue oedema and bone marrow changes but is non-specific. The CRPS Severity Score (CSS) is a validated 17-point scale that quantifies individual symptom and sign severity and tracks treatment response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primary diagnostic tool — evaluates symptoms and signs across all four CRPS domains to confirm diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Detailed clinical history and physical examination by a pain medicine specialist or neurologist. The examiner tests skin temperature, colour, sweating, oedema, allodynia, hyperalgesia, motor strength, and trophic changes. Takes 30-60 minutes. Results are clinician-interpreted; no laboratory markers required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objectively measures skin temperature asymmetry between affected and contralateral limb — asymmetry >1°C supports CRPS diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-contact infrared thermometer is held near the skin surface at multiple points on both limbs. Takes 5-10 minutes. No discomfort. Best measured in a thermostatically controlled room after 20 minutes of acclimatisation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Peri-articular uptake in phase 3 (delayed phase) across multiple joints supports CRPS diagnosis; sensitivity approximately 60-80% for chronic CRPS
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Radiotracer (technetium-99m) injected intravenously; bone images taken immediately, at 2 hours, and at 3-4 hours. Total attendance approximately 4 hours. Mild radiation exposure. Useful for difficult or uncertain diagnoses.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Systematically measures thermal, mechanical, and pressure pain thresholds to objectively characterise sensory abnormalities (allodynia, hyperalgesia, hypoalgesia)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Computerised testing using calibrated thermal probes, von Frey filaments, and pressure algometry applied to the skin. Takes 60-90 minutes in a specialist pain laboratory. No needles or invasive elements.

diagnosticTests.test: Sympathetic blockade response (stellate ganglion or lumbar sympathetic block)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnostic and potentially therapeutic — significant pain relief after sympathetic blockade confirms sympathetically maintained pain (SMP) component
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Image-guided local anaesthetic injection near the sympathetic ganglion under fluoroscopy or ultrasound guidance. Procedure takes 15-30 minutes. Effects assessed over 4-8 hours. Helps select patients likely to respond to further sympatholytic procedures.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows a functional restoration model, with physiotherapy as the core intervention and pharmacotherapy, psychological support, and interventional procedures as adjuncts. The evidence base specifically for CRPS is smaller than for other pain conditions due to diagnostic heterogeneity, but NICE, IASP, and the British Pain Society have published consensus guidelines. Physiotherapy: graded motor imagery (GMI) and mirror visual feedback — progressively retraining the cortical body schema — are the best-evidenced CRPS-specific physiotherapy approaches, reducing pain intensity by 20-35% in systematic reviews. Graded exposure and desensitisation reduce allodynia and kinesiophobia. Pharmacotherapy: no analgesic is specifically licensed for CRPS. Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 30-60 mg/day for 4-8 weeks) are most effective in early inflammatory CRPS and reduce oedema and symptom severity in small RCTs. Bisphosphonates (alendronate, pamidronate, neridronate) reduce bone turnover and pain in CRPS in a meta-analysis of 7 RCTs, with significant NRS reduction. Gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin) address central sensitisation and neuropathic components. Ketamine, at sub-anaesthetic doses by infusion (0.35-0.5 mg/kg/hour over 4-5 days), provides substantial pain relief in 50-70% of treatment-refractory patients in case series and a small RCT. Neuromodulation: spinal cord stimulation (SCS) at the thoracic or cervical level is the most evidence-based interventional treatment, with one RCT (Kemler et al. NEJM 2000) showing SCS superior to physiotherapy alone for pain and quality of life at 6 months. High-frequency stimulation and dorsal root ganglion (DRG) stimulation are newer modalities with emerging evidence. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) is used in refractory CRPS with emerging evidence of benefit in a subset. Multidisciplinary pain rehabilitation programmes integrating all modalities produce the best long-term outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Bisphosphonates (alendronate 40 mg/day for 8 weeks; pamidronate 60 mg IV single dose; or neridronate 100 mg IV in 4 infusions)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce osteoclast activity and bone turnover elevated in CRPS; also modulate inflammatory cytokines. Most consistent evidence for early CRPS with warmth and oedema phase. Meta-analysis of 7 RCTs supports significant pain reduction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NRS reduction of approximately 1.5-2 points at 12 weeks versus placebo; responder rate approximately 50-60% (Breuer et al. 2020 meta-analysis)

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone 30-60 mg/day, tapered over 4-8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Most effective in early-stage warm/inflammatory CRPS within the first 3-6 months. Reduces neurogenic oedema and inhibits mast-cell and immune-mediated inflammation. Monitor blood glucose, blood pressure, and sleep.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant short-term pain and oedema reduction in 2 RCTs; effect wanes beyond 8 weeks; not recommended for chronic cold-phase CRPS

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (600-1,800 mg/day) or pregabalin (150-450 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce central sensitisation and ectopic nerve discharge. Useful for the burning, allodynic, and hyperalgesic components. Commonly prescribed as first-line adjuvant analgesic despite limited CRPS-specific RCT evidence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest pain reduction in neuropathic pain broadly; CRPS-specific evidence from case series and small trials; better tolerability profile than opioids

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose ketamine infusion (0.35-0.5 mg/kg/h IV for 4-5 days inpatient or repeated outpatient sessions)
medicalTreatments.description: NMDA receptor antagonist that blocks central sensitisation. Administered by anaesthetists in inpatient or day-case settings. Patients require monitoring for dissociation, hallucinations, and haemodynamic changes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50-70% of patients with refractory CRPS achieve significant pain reduction at 1 month; some maintain benefit at 3 months; effects often not sustained beyond 6 months without repeat infusions

medicalTreatments.name: Graded motor imagery (GMI) — 6-week programme
medicalTreatments.description: Three-stage rehabilitation programme: limb laterality recognition (brain training), explicit motor imagery (imagining movement), and mirror visual feedback. Progressively retrains the cortical body schema disrupted in CRPS.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 20-35% versus conventional physiotherapy in Moseley RCTs; most effective for upper limb CRPS when all three stages are completed

medicalTreatments.name: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10-50 mg at night)
medicalTreatments.description: Address neuropathic pain components and frequently co-occurring depression and sleep disruption. Titrate slowly from 10 mg to reduce anticholinergic side-effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Level B evidence for neuropathic pain broadly; modest CRPS-specific evidence; improves sleep and mood independently of direct analgesic effect

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS)
surgicalOptions.description: Implanted epidural electrodes at the cervical or thoracic level deliver high-frequency electrical stimulation that inhibits ascending pain signals. The strongest level-1 evidence for CRPS: Kemler et al. NEJM 2000 RCT showed SCS + physiotherapy superior to physiotherapy alone.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant pain relief (>50% reduction) in approximately 50-70% of carefully selected CRPS patients at 2-3 years; response rate is highest in sympathetically maintained pain subtype

surgicalOptions.name: Dorsal root ganglion (DRG) stimulation
surgicalOptions.description: Newer neuromodulation technique placing electrodes at the dorsal root ganglion level for more precise segmental pain coverage. Particularly suitable for focal CRPS of the foot and lower extremity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Composite success (>50% pain reduction plus improved quality of life) in approximately 65-74% at 12 months in the ACCURATE trial (versus 51% for SCS)

alternativeTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)
alternativeTreatments.description: A small RCT (Goebel et al. Pain 2010) showed IVIG 0.5 g/kg produced significantly greater pain reduction than saline in refractory CRPS, with best response in patients with pain duration less than 2 years. Mechanism may involve anti-autoantibody effects.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mirror visual feedback (limb mirror therapy)
alternativeTreatments.description: Patient views the mirror reflection of the unaffected limb performing movements, creating the visual illusion of the affected limb moving normally. Shown to reduce pain in CRPS upper limb cases in systematic reviews; component of GMI, also effective as standalone for mild CRPS.

whySeeSpecialist: CRPS requires specialist pain medicine assessment for Budapest criteria diagnosis, investigation of sympathetically maintained versus sympathetically independent pain, and selection of appropriate interventional therapy. Early specialist referral — within 3-6 months of symptom onset — is strongly associated with better outcomes. Delayed referral beyond 12 months is associated with substantially higher rates of chronic pain and disability.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a pain medicine specialist or anaesthesiologist with documented expertise in CRPS management, ideally within a multidisciplinary pain centre. Verify access to spinal cord stimulator implantation, ketamine infusion protocols, and an integrated rehabilitation team including physiotherapy, psychology, and occupational therapy. CRPS outcomes are significantly better in high-volume specialist centres.
hospitalCriteria: Pain medicine specialist with CRPS diagnosis and management expertise
Fluoroscopy or ultrasound-guided sympathetic block and SCS implantation capability
Inpatient or day-case ketamine infusion facility with anaesthetic monitoring
Multidisciplinary rehabilitation team: physiotherapy, occupational therapy, psychology
Neuromodulation follow-up service for SCS device programming and maintenance
keyFacilities: Tertiary multidisciplinary pain management centres with CRPS specialist programmes
Interventional pain clinics with sympathetic block and neuromodulation services
Rehabilitation medicine units with graded motor imagery-trained physiotherapists
preventionStrategies: Prescribe vitamin C 500 mg/day for 50 days following wrist fracture — two RCTs and a meta-analysis show a 50-75% reduction in CRPS risk after distal radius fracture with this intervention.
Ensure early active physiotherapy after fracture, surgery, or soft-tissue injury — avoid prolonged cast immobilisation beyond clinical necessity.
Screen for catastrophising and anxiety in patients recovering slowly from limb injuries — early psychological support for high-catastrophising individuals reduces central sensitisation progression.
Ensure tight plaster casts are reviewed and adjusted promptly if oedema or disproportionate pain develops — ischaemia from cast pressure is a preventable trigger.
Provide vitamin D supplementation to deficient patients with limb injuries — though evidence is preliminary, deficiency correction is low-risk.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain gentle movement and graded exposure to the affected limb daily — avoidance and guarding behaviour maintain central sensitisation and worsen outcomes.
Practice mirror visual feedback for 10-15 minutes twice daily for upper-limb CRPS as a home programme between clinic appointments.
Engage in a structured pain psychology programme — acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT) or CBT — to address kinesiophobia and catastrophising.
Avoid extremes of temperature to the affected limb, including prolonged cold exposure and hot water immersion, which can trigger or exacerbate allodynia.
Maintain sleep hygiene and treat any identified sleep disorders — poor sleep amplifies central sensitisation and reduces response to all analgesic therapies.
Stay engaged with rehabilitation even on high-pain days — complete cessation of physiotherapy accelerates deconditioning and stiffening of the affected limb.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed, walnuts) to support resolution of neurogenic inflammation
Adequate vitamin D through dietary sources or supplementation — target serum 25-OH vitamin D above 50 nmol/L
Foods rich in magnesium (leafy greens, nuts, legumes) to support NMDA receptor modulation relevant to central sensitisation
Adequate protein intake to maintain muscle mass during rehabilitation and reduced physical activity
avoid: Excess alcohol — interacts with gabapentinoids and tricyclics, worsens central sensitisation, and impairs rehabilitation engagement
Smoking — impairs tissue perfusion and delays healing; nicotine activates sympathetic nervous system tone relevant in sympathetically maintained CRPS
Excess caffeine — increases sympathetic tone and worsens sleep, both aggravating factors in CRPS

exerciseGuidelines: Physiotherapy is central to CRPS management but must be graded to the patient's current functional ability. Start with isometric exercises and graduated passive range of motion; progress to active-assisted, then active exercises. Graded motor imagery precedes physical movement to recalibrate cortical body schema. Hydrotherapy (warm water, not hot) reduces gravitational load and is particularly well tolerated. Overaggressive physiotherapy — known as trauma-triggered exacerbation — worsens some CRPS cases; pacing and graded exposure under specialist supervision are essential.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed physiotherapy and mirror therapy exercises at the same time each day to build routine and track progress.
Use desensitisation techniques daily — progressive gentle texture exposure to the allodynic skin — to habituate central sensitisation.
Protect the affected limb from unintentional trauma and extreme temperatures, but do not immobilise or guard it excessively.
Record pain intensity, medication use, and functional activity in a daily diary to identify patterns and support dose adjustments at clinical review.
Attend regular pain medicine, physiotherapy, and psychology appointments — CRPS management is a coordinated team effort and any single-discipline approach is insufficient.
prognosis: Prognosis is variable and depends heavily on disease duration at treatment initiation. Approximately 74% of patients with acute CRPS show substantial improvement or resolution within 12 months when multidisciplinary treatment begins early. Patients who undergo spinal cord stimulation have significantly better long-term pain outcomes and quality of life compared with physiotherapy alone, though 30-40% of SCS patients experience inadequate response or hardware-related complications over 5 years. Chronic CRPS (more than 2 years' duration) is associated with progressive central sensitisation, fixed dystonia, and significant psychosocial disability; rates of resolution are substantially lower (approximately 30-40% meaningful improvement). Prognostic factors associated with poor outcome include late diagnosis, high initial pain intensity, fixed dystonia, spreading pain beyond the initial limb, and prominent psychiatric comorbidity. Paediatric CRPS has a substantially better prognosis — approximately 80-90% achieve full resolution with intensive rehabilitation within 12 months.
recoveryTimeline: Acute CRPS: 50-70% significant improvement at 6-12 months with early multimodal treatment. After SCS implantation: significant pain reduction usually within days to weeks; stabilisation over 3-6 months. After ketamine infusion: pain relief within days; median duration of benefit 3-6 months. Paediatric CRPS: 80-90% resolution within 6-12 months with intensive rehabilitation.
complications: Fixed dystonia — involuntary sustained abnormal posturing of fingers, wrist, or foot — develops in approximately 25% of CRPS patients with disease duration beyond 1 year; causes significant permanent disability.
Spreading of CRPS to contralateral limb or ipsilateral limb extension, occurring in approximately 7-10% of patients, indicating severe central sensitisation.
Significant functional disability and work loss — approximately 50% of CRPS patients are unable to continue previous employment; long-term disability rates are substantial.
Severe psychiatric comorbidity — depression in up to 50%, anxiety in 70%, suicidal ideation in up to 20% of severe chronic CRPS cases — requires proactive psychological management.
Complications of spinal cord stimulator: lead migration (requiring revision in 10-15%), infection (1-3%), generator failure, and loss of efficacy over 5-10 years.

supportResources.name: IASP — Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: International Association for the Study of Pain authoritative information on CRPS diagnosis, Budapest criteria, and treatment principles.

supportResources.name: RSDSA (Reflex Sympathetic Dystrophy Syndrome Association)
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organisation providing support, peer connections, and CRPS information for patients and families.

supportResources.name: British Pain Society — CRPS Guidance
supportResources.description: UK evidence-based clinical guidance on CRPS diagnosis and management, including Budapest criteria and treatment algorithms.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neuropathic pain from peripheral neuropathy or radiculopathy produces dermatomal or nerve-territory symptoms without the autonomic, vasomotor, and trophic changes that define CRPS. CRPS requires specific Budapest criteria including sudomotor, vasomotor, and motor-trophic features in addition to neuropathic sensory changes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread bilateral pain with fatigue and cognitive symptoms but lacks the regional, limb-specific autonomic and trophic features of CRPS. The two conditions share central sensitisation as a mechanism and can coexist, but are distinct diagnoses with different management pathways.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lumbar radiculopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lumbar radiculopathy produces dermatomal leg pain from nerve root compression with specific reflex and motor changes; it lacks the vasomotor, sudomotor, and trophic features of CRPS. CRPS typically follows a limb injury rather than a spinal pathology, and does not respond to spinal decompression.

confusedWithConditions.name: Postherpetic neuralgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Postherpetic neuralgia follows herpes zoster skin eruption in a dermatomal distribution, with allodynia and burning pain. It lacks the oedema, sweating, colour change, and motor features of CRPS. Treatment is similar (gabapentinoids, tricyclics) but herpes zoster history is the diagnostic differentiator.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic pain
relatedConditions.relevance: CRPS is a neuropathic pain disorder sharing central sensitisation mechanisms and many pharmacological treatment approaches with other neuropathic pain conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Postherpetic neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are chronic neuropathic pain conditions with allodynia and burning pain; managed with similar pharmacological approaches including gabapentinoids and tricyclics.

relatedConditions.name: Central pain syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Central sensitisation is a shared mechanism in both conditions; the cortical and spinal cord changes in chronic CRPS overlap with central pain syndrome pathophysiology.

relatedConditions.name: Phantom limb pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Phantom limb pain shares cortical body-schema disturbance with CRPS; mirror visual feedback was first developed for phantom limb pain before being adapted to CRPS.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic pain syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: CRPS is a specific diagnosis within the broader chronic pain landscape; the psychosocial management principles of chronic pain syndrome apply fully in refractory CRPS.

faqs.question: What is complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS)?
faqs.answer: CRPS is a chronic pain disorder characterised by burning pain, allodynia, autonomic changes (skin temperature and colour), oedema, sweating abnormalities, and motor or trophic changes in a limb following an injury. It is diagnosed using the Budapest criteria. CRPS Type 1 has no identifiable nerve injury; Type 2 follows nerve damage.

faqs.question: What are the Budapest criteria for CRPS?
faqs.answer: The Budapest criteria require pain disproportionate to inciting injury plus symptoms and signs in at least three of four categories: sensory (allodynia, hyperalgesia); vasomotor (temperature or colour asymmetry); sudomotor or oedema (sweating change or swelling); and motor or trophic (weakness, tremor, or abnormal hair or nail growth). Another diagnosis must not better explain the presentation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CRPS Type 1 and Type 2?
faqs.answer: CRPS Type 1 occurs without a confirmed peripheral nerve lesion and accounts for approximately 90% of cases. CRPS Type 2 is associated with an identifiable peripheral nerve injury. Both types share identical clinical features and Budapest diagnostic criteria; management is essentially the same.

faqs.question: What causes CRPS?
faqs.answer: CRPS is most commonly triggered by fractures, surgery, sprains, or crush injuries. Underlying mechanisms include peripheral nociceptor sensitisation, central sensitisation at the spinal cord, sympathetic nervous system dysregulation, and neurogenic inflammation. Not all people who sustain similar injuries develop CRPS; individual susceptibility factors include female sex, anxiety, and prior sensitisation.

faqs.question: How is CRPS diagnosed?
faqs.answer: CRPS is diagnosed clinically using the 2007 Budapest criteria. Objective supporting investigations include infrared thermometry for temperature asymmetry, three-phase bone scintigraphy, and quantitative sensory testing. MRI may show soft-tissue oedema. There is no single blood test or imaging result that diagnoses CRPS.

faqs.question: What are the best treatments for CRPS?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is multidisciplinary: graded motor imagery and physiotherapy for rehabilitation; bisphosphonates, corticosteroids, or gabapentinoids for analgesia; and psychology for catastrophising and kinesiophobia. Spinal cord stimulation has level-1 RCT evidence for refractory CRPS. Ketamine infusion benefits approximately 50-70% of treatment-resistant patients.

faqs.question: Can CRPS spread to other limbs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spreading of CRPS to the contralateral limb or adjacent limb segments occurs in approximately 7-10% of patients with severe or prolonged disease. Spreading indicates significant central sensitisation and is associated with worse prognosis. It is not a new injury but an extension of the same neurological process.

faqs.question: Does CRPS go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Approximately 74% of patients show significant improvement or remission within 12 months with early multimodal treatment. Without treatment, spontaneous remission rates are substantially lower. Chronic CRPS beyond 2 years carries much lower remission rates. Early diagnosis and specialist referral are the strongest predictors of good outcome.

faqs.question: What is graded motor imagery for CRPS?
faqs.answer: Graded motor imagery (GMI) is a three-stage rehabilitation programme: limb laterality discrimination training, explicit motor imagery (vividly imagining movement), and mirror visual feedback. It recalibrates the cortical body schema disrupted in CRPS and reduces pain by 20-35% in clinical trials when all three stages are completed.

faqs.question: Is spinal cord stimulation effective for CRPS?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) is the most evidence-based interventional treatment for CRPS. A landmark RCT (Kemler et al. NEJM 2000) showed SCS plus physiotherapy was significantly superior to physiotherapy alone for pain and quality of life. Approximately 50-70% of carefully selected CRPS patients achieve meaningful pain reduction at 2-3 years.

faqs.question: Can vitamin C prevent CRPS after a fracture?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vitamin C 500 mg daily for 50 days after wrist fracture reduces CRPS risk by approximately 50-75% in two RCTs and a meta-analysis. It is inexpensive, safe, and recommended in international guidelines as prophylaxis after distal radius and ankle fractures.

faqs.question: What is sympathetically maintained pain in CRPS?
faqs.answer: In sympathetically maintained pain (SMP), noradrenaline from sympathetic nerve fibres directly activates sensitised nociceptors, sustaining pain independent of the original injury. SMP is identified by pain relief after sympathetic blockade. It responds to sympatholytic procedures such as stellate ganglion or lumbar sympathetic blocks.

faqs.question: How does CRPS affect children?
faqs.answer: Paediatric CRPS predominantly affects adolescent girls aged 9-15 and most commonly involves the lower extremity. The prognosis is substantially better than adult CRPS — approximately 80-90% achieve full resolution with intensive multidisciplinary rehabilitation within 12 months. Psychological factors are more prominent in paediatric CRPS than in adult disease.

faqs.question: What is ketamine infusion for CRPS?
faqs.answer: Low-dose ketamine infusion (0.35-0.5 mg/kg/hour for 4-5 days) blocks NMDA receptors that sustain central sensitisation in CRPS. It benefits approximately 50-70% of treatment-refractory patients. Effects typically last 3-6 months; repeat infusions are used for sustained benefit. It requires specialist anaesthetic administration and monitoring.

faqs.question: Is CRPS the same as reflex sympathetic dystrophy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reflex sympathetic dystrophy (RSD) and causalgia are older terms for what is now called CRPS Type 1 and Type 2 respectively. The current nomenclature was introduced in 1993 by the IASP to be more diagnostically precise and less mechanistically presumptive. The Budapest criteria formalised in 2003-2007 replaced older diagnostic standards.

symptomKeywords: burning pain in hand or foot
skin hot and red after injury
metaDescription: CRPS: burning limb pain with autonomic changes after injury. Budapest criteria, spinal cord stimulation, ketamine, and graded motor imagery reviewed here.
